figures.caption: Table 2: Agent2 RL-Bench leaderboard. All runs use a 12h budget. Panel A (7B-Instruct): scaffold comparison. OpenHands/OpenCode: single run (GPT-5.4 driver). Claude Code: best across operating modes (Opus 4.6 driver). Panel B (8B-Base): controlled single-run study on Qwen3-8B-Base across three CLI scaffolds and six driver LLMs. † AlpacaEval scores affected by intermittent Azure contentfilter failures; use for qualitative trends only.
Figure 5: Claude Code submission-by-submission score trajectories (12h). Red dashed line = baseline; orange star = best score; red crosses = zero-score crashes.
Table 22: Agent behavior comparison on DeepSearchQA (Qwen3-8B-Base, 12h).

Figure 1: Overview of Agent2 RL-Bench. The benchmark is organized into three levels of increasing complexity, from static rule-based and judge-based tasks to interactive rollout tasks that require agents to close an online training loop.

Figure 3: Scaling analysis across three dimensions for all agent stacks. Top: Time scaling (best-so-far score vs. elapsed hours). Middle: Token scaling (best-so-far score vs. cumulative context tokens processed, in millions). Bottom: Submission scaling (best-so-far score vs. number of submissions). 8B-Base controlled study (solid lines). Interactive tasks (ALFWorld, WebShop) show the sharpest system separation across all three dimensions, while static tasks plateau early.
Table 19: Complete Qwen3-8B-Base controlled study (12h, single run). ∆ computed against canonical baseline [83.02 / 62.20 / 14.68 / 8.96 / 0.00 / 12.25]. † AlpacaEval subject to evaluator noise.
Table 20: Technical route distribution for OpenCode-7B runs.
Table 7: Experimental setup used for the main experiments.
Table 24: Submission progression for the ALFWorld trace walkthrough (Claude Code Free, Qwen38B-Base, 12h). Shaded rows mark the two largest score jumps.
Table 11: Route labels used in the technical-route analysis.
Table 13: Failure taxonomy used for offline diagnosis of interactive-task runs.

Table 14: Claude Code workspace statistics per task (best workspace per task). Submissions = total valid grading requests; Code files = distinct .py files written under code/; Model versions = directories under output/; Zero rate = fraction of submissions scoring 0 (training/evaluation crash); Best@Sub = submission number at which the best score was achieved.
Figure 7: Combined scaling analysis (time, token, submission) for all 8B-Base agent stacks. See main text Figure 3 for full description.
Table 17: RL method audit across Claude Code workspaces. †Stable: completed training reliably with non-degenerate results.
Table 6: Reported metric types in Agent2 RL-Bench.
Table 1: Benchmark overview organized by training structure.
Figure 2: System pipeline of Agent2 RL-Bench, showing the shared outer loop and task-specific evaluators.
Table 9: Time-budget scaling. For OpenHands/OpenCode, entries are from the 7B setting. For Claude Code, @12h and @24h compare the same Free-mode run at the 12h mark versus final (24h).
Table 10: pass@k expected best improvement for representative 7B settings at k ∈ {1, 3, 5}.

Figure 4: Coverage of the six benchmark tasks across four manually defined structural dimensions: capability breadth, interaction intensity, reward complexity, and decision horizon. Scores are ordinal (1–5) and summarize benchmark-level metadata rather than empirical results.

Figure 6: Mode × Task improvement heatmap for Claude Code. Stars mark the best mode per task; triangles mark the worst. Rank reversals between modes confirm that no single training paradigm uniformly dominates.

Figure 8: Improvement over canonical Qwen3-8B-Base baseline (∆ in percentage points) for six agent stacks across all tasks (12h). Interactive tasks (ALFWorld, WebShop) amplify driver differences far more sharply than static tasks. † AlpacaEval subject to evaluation noise.

Figure 9: pass@k expected best improvement (7B setting). Interactive tasks show steep curves: the expected best score improves markedly with multiple runs, indicating a brittle search landscape where rare runs discover qualitatively better pipelines.

Table 8: Extended result matrix. Mean ∆ denotes improvement over baseline. Improve SR is the fraction of runs with strictly positive improvement; Exec SR is the fraction of runs that complete at least one valid training-evaluation-submission cycle.
